_meta.curator: Luke Butcher, Teaching Support, Curtin University
_meta.note: Curated checkpoints per A2030 assessment type. Each checkpoint maps to a specific UDL 3.0 consideration with assessment-tailored harmful and helpful practices written by the curator. Earlier 21-checkpoint generic version is preserved in git history.

io-4-1.title: Vary and honor the methods for response, navigation, and movement
io-4-1.question: For this assessment, do you allow students to adjust their physical environment (seating, position, lighting) and provide flexibility in pace and timing — including extra time for students with adjustment plans?
harmful: Enforce a one-size-fits-all classroom positioning, student placement, sit/stand, response parameters
Prevent additional presentation time for students with Curriculum Adjustment Plans
helpful: Allow students to adjust their physical environment (e.g. flexible seating, classroom positioning, lighting)
Embed flexibility in their means of presentation and response, including digitally (e.g. rate, timing, speed)

io-2-4.title: Address biases in the use of language and symbols
io-2-4.question: Do you actively acknowledge cultural and language differences in delivery — for example recognising students presenting in a second language, enabling closed captioning, and avoiding ableist language?
harmful: No consideration given to ableist or discriminatory language
Not identifying how culture may influence presentation content and delivery
helpful: Acknowledge the different cultures in the room
Recognise students presenting in a second-or-additional language

io-8-3.title: Foster collaboration, interdependence, and collective learning
io-8-3.question: Do you establish group agreements, team roles, and structured collaborative learning goals — and explicitly tell students how they can ask for help?
harmful: Failure to address how students can ask for help
Ignoring practices of socially shared regulation of learning
helpful: Identify team roles, expectations, and collaborative learning goals

fj-3-3.title: Cultivate multiple ways of knowing and making meaning
fj-3-3.question: Do you make scaffolding explicit (chunked information, progressive disclosure) and integrate Indigenous and other holistic knowledge systems where relevant?
harmful: Implying (rather than making explicit) the sequences that scaffold learning
Ignoring holistic and interconnected Indigenous knowledge systems
helpful: Use progressive disclosure of chunked information so students can explore different media
Support a diversity of cognitive approaches to enhance comprehension

fj-5-2.title: Use multiple tools for construction, composition, and creativity
fj-5-2.question: Are students free to use a diversity of analytical and reflective tools, including generative AI with explicit responsible-use guidance?
harmful: Preventing students from using emerging and innovative tools
Failing to teach the foundational and underlying principles that transfer across tools
helpful: Enable a diversity of analytical and reflective tools
Develop students' competencies for responsible use of generative AI
Evaluate students' critique of tools and their synthesis across traditional and contemporary approaches

fj-7-3.title: Nurture joy and play
fj-7-3.question: Do you build in time for imagination, experimentation, and play — and accept a diverse range of artistic and media expressions (music, film, visual, digital, AI-generated)?
harmful: Preventing time for imagination, experimentation, and wonder
Allowing play to occur without self-regulation
Limiting broad sensory and temporal engagement
helpful: Require experimentation across physical and digital spaces
Incorporate requirements for a diverse range of artistic experiences (e.g. music, film, visual, digital, AI-generated)
